summary:
The presence of chronic kidney disease due to vesicoureteral reflux involves progressive structural changes including tubular atrophy and cortial thinning resulting from persistent inflammation and injury over time. These features are consistent with the pathophysiology observed in patients who have had prolonged exposure to urine backflow leading to irreversible tissue damage.

\boxed{C}

The agent correctly identifies that histologic examination in a child with recurrent UTI and Vesicoureteral Reflux (VUR) will show signs of chronic kidney damage such as tubular atrophy and cortical thinning—hallmark features of reflux nephropathy caused by repetitive urodynamic injuries. This finding directly supports Option C based on established pathological patterns linked to longstanding VUR progression. 

\boxed{C} Based on the provided information about a two-year-old female presenting with recurrent urinary tract infections and confirmed vesicoureteral reflux via imaging studies, it follows clinically that her kidneys exhibit characteristic pathology associated with this condition — specifically, chronic interstitial nephritis progressing toward tubular atrophy and cortical thinning due to sustained inflammatory insult from backward-flowing urine. Therefore, the correct histology-based diagnosis aligned with known medical guidelines is $\boxed{\text{C}}$.  

Final Answer: $\boxed{C}$  
(Note: Only final boxed response should appear.)

$\boxed{C}$
